From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Person who speaks at least one variety of Sinitic languages

For the extinct mammal genus, see [Sinophoneus](/wiki/Sinophoneus "Sinophoneus").

Not to be confused with [Sinophobe](/wiki/Anti-Chinese_sentiment "Anti-Chinese sentiment"), [Sinophile](/wiki/Sinophile "Sinophile"), or [Sinosphere](/wiki/Sinosphere "Sinosphere").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Map-Sinophone_World.png/500px-Map-Sinophone_World.png)](/wiki/File:Map-Sinophone_World.png)

Map of the [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese")-speaking world.

Countries and regions with a native Mandarin Chinese-speaking majority

Countries and regions where Mandarin Chinese is not native but an official educational language

Countries with significant Mandarin Chinese-speaking minorities

**Sinophone**, which means "[Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language")-speaking", typically refers to an individual who speaks at least one [variety of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") (that is, one of the [Sinitic languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages")). Academic writers often use the term Sinophone in two definitions: either specifically "Chinese-speaking populations where it is a minority language, excluding [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan")" or generally "Chinese-speaking areas, including where it is an official language".[[1]](#cite_note-1) Many authors use the [collocation](/wiki/Collocation "Collocation") **Sinophone world** or **Chinese-speaking world** to mean the Chinese-speaking world itself (consisting of [Greater China](/wiki/Greater_China "Greater China") and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")) or the distribution of the [Chinese diaspora](/wiki/Chinese_diaspora "Chinese diaspora") outside of Greater China.

[Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") is the most commonly spoken variety of the Chinese languages today, with over 1 billion total speakers (approximately 12% of the world population), of which about 900 million are native speakers, making it the [most spoken first language in the world](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_number_of_native_speakers "List of languages by number of native speakers") and [second most spoken overall](/wiki/List_of_languages_by_total_number_of_speakers "List of languages by total number of speakers").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It is the official variety of Chinese in mainland China, Taiwan, and Singapore. Meanwhile, [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") is the official variety of Chinese in Hong Kong and Macau and is also widely spoken among significant [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") communities in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") as well as the rest of the world.

The [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") of [Sinophone](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Sinophone "wikt:Sinophone") stems from *[Sino-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Sino- "wikt:Sino-")* "China; Chinese" (cf. [Sinology](/wiki/Sinology "Sinology")) and *[-phone](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-phone "wikt:-phone")* "speaker of a certain language" (e.g. [Anglophone](/wiki/Anglophone "Anglophone"), [Francophone](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone")).

Edward McDonald (2011) claimed the word *sinophone* "seems to have been coined separately and simultaneously on both sides of the Pacific" in 2005, by [Geremie Barmé](/wiki/Geremie_Barm%C3%A9 "Geremie Barmé") of [Australia National University](/wiki/Australia_National_University "Australia National University") and [Shu-mei Shih](/wiki/Shu-mei_Shih "Shu-mei Shih") of [UCLA](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles"). Barmé (2008) explained the "Sinophone world" as "one consisting of the individuals and communities who use one or another—or, indeed, a number—of China-originated languages and dialects to make meaning of and for the world, be it through speaking, reading, writing or via an engagement with various electronic media." Shih (2004:29) noted, "By 'sinophone' literature I mean literature written in Chinese by Chinese-speaking writers in various parts of the world outside China, as distinguished from 'Chinese literature'—literature from China."

Nevertheless, there are two earlier *sinophone* usages. Ruth Keen (1988:231) defined "Sinophone communities" in [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature") as "the Mainland, Taiwan, Hong Kong, Singapore, Indonesia and the U.S." Coulombe and Roberts (2001:12) compared students of [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") between *[anglophones](/wiki/English_language "English language")* "with English as their mother tongue" and *[allophones](/wiki/Allophone_(Quebec) "Allophone (Quebec)")* (in the [Quebec English](/wiki/Quebec_English "Quebec English") sense) "without English or French as their mother tongue", including *sinophones* defined as "Cantonese/Mandarin speakers".

The *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* does not yet include *Sinophone*, but records 1900 as the earliest usage of the [French loanwords](/wiki/List_of_English_words_of_French_origin "List of English words of French origin") *Francophone* for "French-speaking" and *Anglophone* for "English-speaking". The [French language](/wiki/French_language "French language") – which first used *Sinophone* to mean "Chinese-speaking" in 1983 (CNRTL 2012) – differentiates *[Francophone](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Francophone "wikt:Francophone")* meaning "French-speaking, especially in a region where two or more languages are spoken" and *[Francophonie](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Francophonie "wikt:Francophonie")* "French-speaking, collectively, the French-speaking world" (commonly abbreviating the [Organisation internationale de la Francophonie](/wiki/Organisation_internationale_de_la_Francophonie "Organisation internationale de la Francophonie")). [Haun Saussy](/wiki/Haun_Saussy "Haun Saussy") contrasted the English lexicon lacking an inclusive term like *Sinophonie* or *Sinophonia*, and thus using *Sinophone* to mean both "Chinese-speaking, especially in a region where it is a minority language" and "all Chinese-speaking areas, including [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Chinese-speaking world](/wiki/Chinese-speaking_world "Chinese-speaking world")".

> "Sinophone" operates as a calque on "Francophone", as the application of the logic of Francophonie to the domain of Chinese extraterritorial speech. But that analogy is sure to hiccup, like all analogies, at certain points. Some, but not all, Francophone regions are populated by descendants of French emigrants, as virtually all of Sinophonia (I think) is populated by descendants of Chinese emigrants. Other regions, the majority in both area and population, are Francophone as a result of conquest or enslavement. That might be true of some areas of China too, but in a far more distant past. And at another level, the persistence of French had to do with the exportation of educational protocols by the Grande Nation herself, something that wasn't obviously true of the Middle Kingdom in recent decades but now, with the Confucius Institutes, is perhaps taking form. (2012)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

English *Sinophonia* was the theme of an international conference organized by Christopher Lupke, President of the Association of Chinese and Comparative Literature, and hosted by Peng Hsiao-yen, Senior Researcher in the Institute of Chinese Literature and Philosophy, ([Academia Sinica](/wiki/Academia_Sinica "Academia Sinica") 2012) on "Global Sinophonia" – Chinese *Quanqiu Huayu Wenhua* 全球華語文化 (literally "global Chinese-language culture").

## Usages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Usages")]

In the two decades since the English word *sinophone* was coined, it has gone through [semantic change](/wiki/Semantic_change "Semantic change") and increasing usage. Authors currently use it in at least two meanings, the general sense of "Chinese-speaking", and the academic "Chinese-speaking, especially in areas where it is a minority language." [Shu-mei Shih](/wiki/Shu-mei_Shih "Shu-mei Shih"), one of the leading academic authorities on Sinophone scholarship, summarized treatments.

> In the past few years, scholars have used the term *Sinophone* for largely denotative purposes to mean "Chinese-speaking" or "written in Chinese". Sau-ling Wong used it to designate Chinese American literature written in "Chinese" as opposed to English ("Yellow"); historians of the Manchu empire such as Pamela Kyle Crossley, Evelyn S. Rawski, and Jonathan Lipman described "Chinese-speaking" Hui Muslims in China as Sinophone Muslims as opposed to Uyghur Muslims, who speak Turkic languages; Patricia Schiaini- Vedani and Lara Maconi distinguished between Tibetan writers who write in the Tibetan script and "Chinese-language", or Sinophone, Tibetan writers. Even though the main purpose of these scholars' use of the term is denotative, their underlying intent is to clarify contrast by naming: in highlighting a Sinophone Chinese American literature, Wong exposes the anglophone bias of scholars and shows that American literature is multilingual; Crossley, Rawski, and Lipman emphasize that Muslims in China have divergent languages, histories, and experiences; Schiaini- Vedani and Maconi suggest the predicament of Tibetan writers who write in the "language of the colonizer" and whose identity is bound up with linguistic difference. (2013:8)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### General meanings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: General meanings")]

"Chinese-speaking" is the [literal meaning](/wiki/Literal_meaning "Literal meaning") of *sinophone*, without the academic distinction of speakers outside of [Greater China](/wiki/Greater_China "Greater China").

The *[Wiktionary](/wiki/Wiktionary "Wiktionary")* is one of the few dictionaries that define *[sinophone](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sinophone "wikt:sinophone")*:

* **adjective** "Speaking one or more Sinitic or Chinese language(s), Chinese-speaking"
* **noun** "a person who speaks one or more of the Sinitic or Chinese language(s) either natively or by adoption, a Chinese-speaking person."

### Academic meanings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Academic meanings")]

The word *sinophone* has different meanings among scholars in fields such as [Sinology](/wiki/Sinology "Sinology"), [linguistics](/wiki/Linguistics "Linguistics"), [comparative literature](/wiki/Comparative_literature "Comparative literature"), [language teaching](/wiki/Language_teaching "Language teaching"), and [postcolonialism](/wiki/Postcolonialism "Postcolonialism").

Recent definitions of the word include:

* The Sinophone encompasses Sinitic-language communities and their expressions (cultural, political, social, etc.) on the margins of nations and nationalness in the internal colonies and other minority communities in China as well as outside it, with the exception of settler colonies where the Sinophone is the dominant vis-à-vis their indigenous populations. (Shih 2011:716)
* The Sinophone world refers to Sinitic-language cultures and communities born of colonial and postcolonial histories on the margins of geopolitical nation-states all across the world. (Cambria 2012)

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Official_Chinese_language_in_the_World.svg/250px-Official_Chinese_language_in_the_World.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Official_Chinese_language_in_the_World.svg)

Countries and territories in which a variety of Chinese is an official language.

Sole official language

Co-official language

### Chinese-speaking countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Chinese-speaking countries")]

Main article: [List of countries and territories where Chinese is an official language](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_where_Chinese_is_an_official_language "List of countries and territories where Chinese is an official language")

Chinese is an [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of five countries and territories. While Chinese is a group of related languages rather than a single language itself, the governments of nearly all nations and territories where it is official simply designate the ambitious "Chinese" to refer to the official variant used in administration and education, with the exception of Singapore.[[5]](#cite_note-statutes.agc.gov.sg-5)

[Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") is the sole official language of both the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC) and the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China") (ROC, Taiwan) as well as one of the four official languages of [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). It is also one of the six [official languages of the United Nations](/wiki/Official_languages_of_the_United_Nations "Official languages of the United Nations").

[Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") is an official language of [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") (alongside [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") respectively), where it is the dominant variety of Chinese rather than Mandarin.

### Overseas communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Overseas communities")]

[Overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") and Chinese-speaking communities are found worldwide, with the most sizable concentrated in much of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and some countries in the Western World, particularly the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and [France](/wiki/France "France"). The [usage and varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Language_and_overseas_Chinese_communities "Language and overseas Chinese communities") among the Chinese [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora") is usually dependent on various factors, mostly the ancestral region of the dominant Chinese group and official language policy of the country of residence. In Southeast Asia, Cantonese and [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") are the dominant [variants of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"), with the former traditionally serving as a *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")* amongst most ethnic Chinese in the region.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988191-6) In Western countries with large ethnic Chinese populations, more established Chinese communities use Cantonese or [Taishanese](/wiki/Taishanese "Taishanese"), although Mandarin is increasingly spoken by newer arrivals.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") is the only country outside of the Chinese-speaking world that permits the usage of Chinese as a medium of instruction.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This is largely influenced by the fact that [Malaysian Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") comprise nearly a quarter of the country's population and have traditionally been highly influential in the country's economic sector.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGomez2012185-9) While Mandarin is the variant of Chinese used in Chinese-language schools, speakers of Hokkien form a plurality in the ethnic Chinese population and Cantonese serves as the common language, especially in commerce and media.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWurmMühlhäuslerTryon2011698-10)

### As a foreign language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: As a foreign language")]

Main article: [Chinese as a foreign language](/wiki/Chinese_as_a_foreign_language "Chinese as a foreign language")

With the economic and political rise of the Sinophone world since the latter half of the 20th century, particularly China itself starting in the 1980s, Mandarin Chinese has increasingly become a popular [foreign language](/wiki/Language_education "Language education") throughout the world.[[11]](#cite_note-11) While not as widespread as a standard foreign language at the scale of English, French, Spanish, or German, student enrollment rates and courses in Mandarin have rapidly grown in [East](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and Western countries.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Besides standard Mandarin, Cantonese is the only other Chinese language that is widely taught as a foreign language, in part due to the global economic importance of Hong Kong and its widespread presence in significant Overseas Chinese communities.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sinophone&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Statistics")]

*[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* estimates the total number of Sinophones at about 1.4 billion worldwide as of 2020, the vast majority (1.3 billion) of whom are native speakers.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The most spoken branch of Chinese is [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") with 1.12 billion speakers (921 million native speakers), followed by [Yue](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") (which includes [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")) with 85 million speakers (84 million native). Other branches of the Chinese language subgroup with over 2 million speakers include: [Wu](/wiki/Wu_Chinese "Wu Chinese") with 82 million (81.7 million native), [Min Nan](/wiki/Southern_Min "Southern Min") with 49 million (48.4 million native), [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") with 48.2 million, [Jin](/wiki/Jin_Chinese "Jin Chinese") with 47 million, [Xiang](/wiki/Xiang_Chinese "Xiang Chinese") with 37.3 million, [Gan](/wiki/Gan_Chinese "Gan Chinese") with 22.1 million, [Min Bei](/wiki/Northern_Min "Northern Min") with 11 million, [Min Dong](/wiki/Eastern_Min "Eastern Min") with 10.3 million, [Huizhou](/wiki/Huizhou_Chinese "Huizhou Chinese") with 4.6 million, and [Pu-Xian Min](/wiki/Pu-Xian_Min "Pu-Xian Min") with 2.5 million.
